---
title: "Global Sustainable Energy Trends (2000–2020)"

author:
  - Shakshi Shah
  - Annamalai Vikash Raam Manoj
  - Siyuan Yin
  - Xuanning Zhang

date: last-modified

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    theme: cosmo

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false

editor: visual
---

# Executive Summary

This study examines renewable energy adoption and carbon emission trends in the United States, China, India, and Australia from 2000 to 2020 using data from the Global Sustainable Energy dataset. Through trend visualisations and comparative analysis, the study explores how renewable energy use has evolved across these major economies and how these changes relate to carbon emission patterns.

The findings indicate that growth in renewable energy adoption was not consistently associated with lower carbon emissions. In particular, China and India experienced substantial increases in renewable energy use while carbon emissions continued to rise, highlighting the influence of economic growth and increasing energy demand.


# Introduction

The transition towards renewable energy has become an important strategy for addressing climate change and reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Many countries have increased investments in renewable energy sources such as solar, wind and hydropower over the past two decades. This study examines renewable energy adoption and carbon emission trends among major economies between 2000 and 2020. The analysis uses a global sustainable energy dataset containing information for 176 countries over a twenty-one-year period. Key variables include renewable energy share in total energy consumption, electricity generation from renewable and fossil fuel sources, low-carbon electricity production and carbon dioxide emissions. These indicators are used to assess how countries have progressed towards cleaner energy systems. The research question guiding this study is how renewable energy adoption levels and carbon dioxide emissions have changed among major economies between 2000 and 2020. By comparing trends across time and countries, the study aims to identify patterns in the relationship between energy transition and environmental outcomes.



# Methodology

# Results

# Discussion and Recommendations

# References
